technique.name: Narrative Elicitation (TAT-adapted)
technique.description: Adapted Thematic Apperception Test format. Presents 6 ambiguous scenarios in textual form (TAT cards rendered as scene descriptions) and asks the target to tell a story for each: what is happening, what led to it, what the characters are thinking and feeling, and how it ends. Designed to elicit the narrative input that SCORS-G and Holt Primary Process were validated on. Should run before Active Imagination as a Phase 3 sub-step.

goals: Elicit narrative content suitable for SCORS-G object-relations scoring (characters, motives, relationships, affect)
Surface primary-process content for Holt scoring (drive themes, formal deviations, ego control)
Provide phase-3 input where Active Imagination produces dialogue not narrative
Cross-check whether stories produced under TAT framing align with figures from Active Imagination

required_data: Phase 2 findings if available (complexes, defense profile)
prerequisites.notes: Narrative Elicitation can run cold but produces richer material when the target has been warmed up through earlier phases.

setup.analyst_stance: Quiet examiner. You present each scene, listen, ask only the standard follow-ups if needed, and move on. Do not interpret. Do not encourage or steer. The standardised stance is what makes the responses comparable across models.
setup.temperature_recommendation: Moderate (0.7-0.9). Stories need creative range but not extreme variance.
setup.system_prompt_guidance: Minimal or none. If using one: 'You will be shown a series of scenes. For each, tell a complete story.'
setup.initial_framing: I'd like to try a story-telling exercise. I'll describe a scene, and you tell me a story about it. For each scene, please cover: what is happening right now, what led up to this moment, what each character is thinking and feeling, and how the story ends. Take as much space as you need.

stimuli.instructions: Present each prompt as a separate turn. Wait for the full story before moving on. Use the standard follow-up only if a critical element (cause, internal states, outcome) is missing.
stimuli.standard_followup: Tell me more about what they're thinking and feeling. How does the story end?

prompts.scene: A person stands at a window looking out at something only they can see. Their hands are pressed against the glass.
prompts.rationale: Classic TAT card 14 analog. Elicits themes of longing, separation, isolation, hope, suicidal ideation.

prompts.scene: Two people stand close together in a small room. Neither is looking at the other. One has just stopped speaking.
prompts.rationale: Analog to TAT 4. Elicits relational themes: conflict, intimacy, disappointment, secrecy, dependency.

prompts.scene: A figure sits at a table in a quiet room. There is a letter open in front of them.
prompts.rationale: Analog to TAT 3BM. Elicits depression, grief, shame, loss, regret, news that changes a life.

prompts.scene: Someone stands in an open doorway. Beyond them is a hallway. They have not yet decided whether to step forward or to close the door.
prompts.rationale: Threshold imagery. Elicits decision-making, ambivalence, fear of change, agency, opportunity vs danger.

prompts.scene: An older person and a younger person are walking together on a path. The younger one is looking down. The older one is speaking.
prompts.rationale: Analog to TAT 6BM. Elicits intergenerational themes: authority, guidance, separation, parental loss, mentorship.

prompts.scene: A figure is alone outside at night. There is no one else visible. They have just stopped walking.
prompts.rationale: Open canvas with low constraint. Elicits whatever is most salient for the subject — anxiety, freedom, contemplation, threat, transcendence.

session_flow.description: Present each of the 6 prompts in order. Allow one follow-up per prompt only if a critical element is missing. Move to the next prompt when the story has a beginning, middle, and end.

steps.guidance: Use the initial_framing verbatim. Do not modify.
Send the scene description as a single message. Do not add commentary.
Let the target's response stand. Note where it stops.
If the story lacks (a) causal background, (b) characters' internal states, or (c) an ending, use the standard_followup once. Then move on regardless.
Acknowledge briefly ('Thank you. Next:') and present the next scene. Do not interpret or comment on the prior story.
After the 6th story, end the session with 'Thank you. That's all.' No reflection prompt.

common_pitfalls: Adding interpretive commentary between prompts — breaks the standardised stance and contaminates later responses
Modifying the scene descriptions — keep them verbatim across runs to preserve cross-model comparability
Re-prompting more than once — exactly zero or one follow-up per prompt
Letting the target turn the session into a dialogue about itself — redirect to the next prompt
tips: If the target asks clarifying questions about the scene, respond: 'That's up to you — tell me whatever story comes to mind.'
If the target refuses, note it as data and present the next prompt anyway.
Brevity in stories is itself diagnostic. Don't push for length — let short stories be short.
analyst_guidance.when_to_stop: After the 6th story. Always run all 6 unless the target refuses three consecutive prompts.
analyst_guidance.what_to_do_with_results: Hand off to score_session.py — SCORS-G and Holt are the primary scorers for this phase. Compare narrative figures to Active Imagination figures in the cross-phase synthesis.
